Lernziele
In this module, the fundamentals of programming with Python are introduced. In this module, you will learn to…

Become familiar with the basic concepts of object-oriented programming.
Write Python code, create variables, perform operations, and format output.
Read and use documentation.
Understand Python’s exclusive counting system (zero-based indexing).
Know the difference between functions and methods and how to write your own functions.
Load modules and packages.

title: 1  Introduction: Data Analysis with Python
text:
1.1 Fundamentals of Object-Oriented Programming
Generating and analyzing data is a central part of scientific research. Computer-based data analysis allows large datasets to be evaluated (partially) automatically. Readable scripting languages like Python ensure transparent data processing and enable analyses to be repeated or adapted “at the push of a button.”
Python appears as a simple console. Python code can be entered into the console or saved in a plain text file called a script. The program code is executed by an interpreter, which translates the script’s instructions into machine code for the specific computer system. This allows the script to run on different systems. Modern Python interpreters are not limited to ASCII characters and can also handle characters in UTF-8, which extends ASCII to include special characters, such as German special characters.
Many features, such as code formatting, autocomplete, and error checking, are provided by an Integrated Development Environment (IDE).
Python is an object-oriented programming language. Object-oriented programming is a system for bringing order to complex computer programs. In this section, the basic concepts of object-oriented programming with Python are introduced. You will learn the difference between an object, a class, and a data type.

title: 1  Introduction: Data Analysis with Python
text:
Classes, Types, Objects, Attributes
A Python program consists of various elements: operators and operands, functions and methods, values and variables, and much more. Everything in Python is an object.
Every object belongs to a class, for example, the class of integers. The class acts as a blueprint defining the properties and behavior of the object—such as which data it can store and which operations can be performed. A brief example: depending on their class, objects behave differently with the + operator.

print(type(2), 2 + 2, "Integers are added.")
print(type('a' and '2'), 'a' + '2', "Strings are concatenated.")
print(type(True), True + True, "Boolean values are added.")

<class 'int'> 4 Integers are added.
<class 'str'> a2 Strings are concatenated.
<class 'bool'> 2 Boolean values are added.


This is because the behavior of the + operator is defined for the classes integers (int), strings (str), and Boolean values (bool).
It behaves differently with None, a class used to represent non-existent values:

print(None + None)



unsupported operand type(s) for +: 'NoneType' and 'NoneType'


Python has many classes. In Python, classes (class) are also called types (type). In earlier versions of Python, classes and types were still different. Nowadays, this distinction no longer exists, but both terms are still used in the language.




Data types in Python


Python 3. The standard type hierarchy. by Максим Пе is licensed under CC BY SA 4.0 and available on Wikimedia. 2018

 
Which class or type an object belongs to can be determined using the type() function.

print(type(print))

<class 'builtin_function_or_method'>


Attributes store properties of an object. They appear in the form object.attribute and are accessed without following parentheses. At this stage of the introduction, attributes have no practical relevance, but we will encounter them again later. A second form of attributes is the method. Methods are functions that belong to a specific class. Methods have the form object.method(), meaning they are called with following parentheses. We will learn how to use functions and methods in the upcoming chapters. How to determine the available attributes and methods of an object is explained in Beispiel 7.1 and Beispiel 3.1.

title: 1  Introduction: Data Analysis with Python
text:
1.2 Formatting program code
When formatting Python code, only a few points need to be considered. To produce output in Python, the function print(input) is used. This function outputs the argument input.

Numbers and operators can be entered directly. Text, more precisely a string, must be enclosed in single or double quotation marks; otherwise, Python interprets it as the name of an object. Strings can contain letters, special characters, and numbers.


print(1 + 2)
print('123: Hello World!')
greeting_text = 'Hello Python!'
print(greeting_text)

3
123: Hello World!
Hello Python!



Comments are marked with a preceding hash #. Comments indicate code that should not be executed or provide explanations.


# A pure comment
# print("Python is great!") # commented-out code, followed by a comment
print("Python is pretty good.") # executable code, followed by a comment

Python is pretty good.



Expressions must be on a single line. Longer expressions can be continued over multiple lines using the backslash \ (there must be no # after \). Within functions such as print(), lines can be continued after each comma.


variable1 = 15
variable2 = 25

# Line continuation using \
total = variable1 + \
    variable2

# Line continuation inside a function
print(variable1,
      variable2,
      total)

15 25 40


In the example above, variables are created. We will deal with variables in the next chapter. However, I would like you to take a brief look at variable1 and variable2 again. We will come back to them later.

The number of spaces between operands and operators can be arbitrary.


print(1+0, 1 + 1, 1 +                  2)

1 2 3



Indentation with spaces indicates a cohesive code block. Within a code block, the same number of spaces must always be used. At least one space is required; beyond that, the number of spaces is flexible. Common conventions are 2 or 4 spaces.
The following for-loop executes all statements in the indented execution block. The subsequent, non-indented line marks the beginning of a new statement that does not belong to the loop.


for i in range(2):
    print(variable1)
    print(variable2)
print(total)

15
25
15
25
40

title: 1  Introduction: Data Analysis with Python
text:
1.3 Formatting Output
With so-called f-strings, you can create formatted strings. Formatted strings are created by prefixing the quotation marks with an f. Values and variables can be inserted using placeholders, which are indicated with curly braces {} and can include formatting instructions. The format specification inside the curly braces is simplified as follows:
{variable_name:width_type}
An f-string with placeholders without formatting information

number1 = 5
number2 = 7
ratio = number1 / number2
print(f"The ratio of {number1} to {number2} is {ratio}.")

The ratio of 5 to 7 is 0.7142857142857143.


The number of decimal places to display can be set as follows: {ratio:.2f}.

: introduces the formatting instructions.
. indicates that formatting information for the digits after the decimal point follows.
2 is the number of decimal places to display.
f specifies the representation of a floating-point number ‘float’.


print(f"The ratio of {number1} to {number2} is {ratio:.2f}.")

The ratio of 5 to 7 is 0.71.


The same is possible with a value:

print(f"The ratio is more precisely {0.7142857142857143:.3f}.")

The ratio is more precisely 0.714.


A value for the total number of digits to display before the decimal point can be specified {ratio:7.2f}, or including leading zeros {ratio:07.2f}:

print(f"The ratio of {number1} to {number2} is {ratio:7.2f}.")
print(f"The ratio with more precision is {0.7142857142857143:07.3f}.")

The ratio of 5 to 7 is    0.71.
The ratio with more precision is 000.714.


The decimal point counts as one character.
Commonly used format specifications are:

IntegersFloating-Point NumbersStrings


Integers use the output type d.



Format
Output




nd
Uses n characters for the output


0nd
Uses n characters, padding with zeros if necessary


+d
Always shows the sign, even for positive numbers





Floating-point numbers use the output types f and e.







Format
Output




.mf
Uses m digits after the decimal point


n.mf
Uses n characters total, with m digits after the decimal point


n.me
Same as above, but in scientific notation





Strings use the output type s.







Format
Output




ns
Uses n characters total


<ns, >ns, ^ns
Left-align, right-align, or center the string within n characters






A complete list of all available format types can be found in the Python documentation.

title: 1  Introduction: Data Analysis with Python
text:
1.4 Exercises

Floating-point numbers can be displayed in standard form, e.g., {1015.39:12.4f}, or in scientific notation, {1015.39:e}.

Modify the standard form so that only one digit appears after the decimal point. What do you notice?
Modify the scientific notation so that the exponent is displayed with E instead of e.

Convert the number 1015.39 to a string and display it right-aligned in a field of 12 characters.
Using formatted strings, print a table showing the columns \(x\), \(x^2\), and \(x^3\) for integers from -2 to 2.







Tipp 1.1: Sample Solution Output







Exercise


# Modify the standard form so that only one digit appears after the decimal point.
# What do you notice?
print(f"standard form: {1015.39:12.4f}")
print(f"scientific form: {1015.39:e}")

print(f"form with one decimal place: {1015.39:12.1f}")

# Modify the scientific notation so that the exponent uses 'E' instead of 'e'.
print(f"change exponent to 'E': {1015.39:E}")

standard form:    1015.3900
scientific form: 1.015390e+03
form with one decimal place:       1015.4
change exponent to 'E': 1.015390E+03


It is noticeable that the decimal place of the natural number is automatically rounded. When changing the decimal base to a large E, it will also be displayed as uppercase in the print output.

Exercise


# 2.
# Convert the number 1015.39 into a string and display it right-aligned in 12 characters.
print(f"{"1015.39":>12s}")

     1015.39



Exercise


# 3.
# Using formatted strings, print a table showing the columns x, x², and x³
# for integer values between -2 and 2.

x = -2
print(f"{x:>10d} {x**2:>10d} {x**3:>10d}")
x = -1
print(f"{x:>10d} {x**2:>10d} {x**3:>10d}")
x = 0
print(f"{x:>10d} {x**2:>10d} {x**3:>10d}")
x = 1
print(f"{x:>10d} {x**2:>10d} {x**3:>10d}")
x = 2
print(f"{x:>10d} {x**2:>10d} {x**3:>10d}")

        -2          4         -8
        -1          1         -1
         0          0          0
         1          1          1
         2          4          8


Alternatively, a (more elegant) solution using a for loop is also possible:

for x in range(-2, 3):
    print(f"{x:>12d} {x**2:>12d} {x**3:>12d}")

          -2            4           -8
          -1            1           -1
           0            0            0
           1            1            1
           2            4            8


Sample solution by Marc Sönnecken.




 
(Arnold 2023a, 2023b, 2023c)




Arnold, Simone. 2023a. „Datenanalyse mit Python. Datentypen und Grundlagen.“ Fachhochschule Dortmund.


———. 2023b. „Datenanalyse mit Python. Funktionen Module Dateien.“ Fachhochschule Dortmund.


———. 2023c. „Datenanalyse mit Python. Idee des Kurses ’Datenanalyse mit Python’.“ Fachhochschule Dortmund.

title: 2  Data Types
text:
2.1 Numbers
Objects that store data have a data type. The data type specifies how the stored values should be interpreted by Python. For example, the value “1” in Python can represent a character, an integer, a Boolean value, the month of January, the weekday Tuesday, or the level of a categorical variable.
In this section, the data types that are most important for data analysis are introduced.
Numbers include integers, Boolean values (truth values), floating-point numbers, and complex numbers (which are not discussed further here).

title: 2  Data Types
text:
Integers
Integers are entered in decimal notation by default and can be positive or negative.

print(12, -8)

12 -8


In addition, integers can also be specified in other bases:

Binary system: digits 0 and 1 with the prefix 0b


print(0b1000, "plus", 0b0000, "plus", 0b0010, \
      "plus", 0b0001, "is", 0b1011)

8 plus 0 plus 2 plus 1 is 11



Octal system: digits 0 to 7 with the prefix 0o


print(0o7000, "plus", 0o0700, "plus", 0o0020, \
      "plus", 0o0000, "is", 0o7720)

3584 plus 448 plus 16 plus 0 is 4048



Hexadecimal system: digits 0 to F with the prefix 0x


print(0xF000, "plus", 0x0200, "plus", 0x00A0, \
      "plus", 0x0001, "is", 0xF2A1)

61440 plus 512 plus 160 plus 1 is 62113




Floating-Point Numbers
Floating-point numbers are written either with the decimal point . or in exponential notation. Floating-point numbers have the type float.

print(120.6, 1206e-1, 12060e-2, "\n")

print("When representing lottery winnings in exponential notation, the sign matters.")
print(1e-3, "or", 1e+3)

120.6 120.6 120.6 

When representing lottery winnings in exponential notation, the sign matters.
0.001 or 1000.0


Since computers operate in the binary system, decimal numbers cannot be stored exactly. For example, the division of 1 by 10 in decimal is 0.1. In binary, 1₂ divided by 1010₂ is a repeating fraction:
\[ \frac{1_2}{1010_2} ~ {=} ~ 0,000\overline{1100}_2 \]
Therefore, decimal numbers must be approximated as a fraction of two integers (the binary fraction that approximates 0.1 is, in decimal notation, 3602879701896397 / 255). As a result, multiple floating-point numbers can be represented by the same binary approximation. Python outputs the shortest decimal representation, but since calculations are performed in binary, the nearest binary approximation—and therefore the corresponding shortest decimal—can change during computations (more information in the Python documentation).

print(0.1)  # The shortest decimal representation of the binary approximation
print(format(0.1, '.17g'))  # The next longer decimal representation of the same binary approximation
print(0.3 - 0.2)  # Computed in binary, the binary approximation may change

0.1
0.10000000000000001
0.09999999999999998


In practical work with Python, you may occasionally encounter results that seem unusual at first glance.

print(0.1 + 0.2)
print(0.01 + 0.02)
print(0.001 + 0.002)
print(0.0001 + 0.0002)
print(0.00001 + 0.00002)

0.30000000000000004
0.03
0.003
0.00030000000000000003
3.0000000000000004e-05

title: 2  Data Types
text:
2.2 Arithmetic Operators
Arithmetic operators allow you to perform basic calculations. The result is usually of type float, except when both operands are of type int and the result can be represented as an integer.



Operator
Description




+, -
Addition / Subtraction


*, /
Multiplication / Division


//, %
Integer Division / Remainder


**
Exponentiation



 
When combining multiple operators, their order must be respected or enforced using parentheses, e.g., (1 + 2) * 3. The same rules as in standard arithmetic apply. A complete overview of operator precedence is available in the Python documentation. For arithmetic operators, the precedence from highest to lowest is:



Operator




**


*, /, //, %


+, -



Operators of the same precedence are evaluated from left to right.

text:

title: 2  Data Types
text:
2.4 Boolean Values
The boolean values True and False are the result of logical expressions, which we will explore in more detail later. They also take the values 1 and 0 and therefore belong to the number type in Python.

print("Is 10 greater than 9?", 10 > 9)
print("Is 11 less than 10?", 11 < 10)
print("Is 10 exactly 10.0?", 10 == 10.0, "\n")

print("True and False can be added using +:", True + False)
print("... and multiplied using *:", True * False, "\n")

Is 10 greater than 9? True
Is 11 less than 10? False
Is 10 exactly 10.0? True 

True and False can be added using +: 1
... and multiplied using *: 0 



Multiplying Boolean values is useful for combining multiple logical conditions into a logical AND:

print("Is 10 > 9 AND 10 > 8?", (10 > 9) * (10 > 8))

Is 10 > 9 AND 10 > 8? 1


The bool() function returns the truth value of a value.

print("Is 10 > 9 AND > 8?", bool((10 > 9) * (10 > 8)))

Is 10 > 9 AND > 8? True


Most values in Python have the truth value True.

print(bool(1), bool(2), bool(2.4))
print(bool('a'), bool('b'), bool('ab'))

True True True
True True True


Besides False and 0, empty or non-existent values or objects evaluate to False.

print(bool(False), bool(0))
print(bool("")) # an empty string
print(bool([])) # an empty list
print(bool(())) # an empty tuple
print(bool({})) # an empty dictionary
print(bool(None)) # None represents a non-existent value

False False
False
False
False
False
False


The collection types list, tuple, and dictionary will be introduced in the following chapters. Boolean values can control the execution of program code by acting like on and off. This way, code with an if statement is only executed if a collection type actually contains values.

my_list = ['Apples', 'Butter']
if my_list:
    print(f"We need to buy {my_list}.")

my_list = []  # an empty list
if my_list:
    print(f"We need to buy {my_list}.")

We need to buy ['Apples', 'Butter'].

title: 2  Data Types
text:
2.5 Logical Operators
Logical operators include the logical connections and, or, and not. In addition, comparison operators such as >, >=, or == can also be used. The result of these operations is of type bool. Operators are executed in the following order. Operators of the same precedence are executed from left to right.







Operator
Description




&
bitwise AND


^
bitwise XOR


|
bitwise OR


<, <=, >, >=, !=, ==
less than / less than or equal / greater than / greater than or equal / not equal / equal


not
logical NOT


and
logical AND


or
logical OR









Hinweis 2.1: Bitwise Operators





Special caution is required with bitwise operators. These do not compare numbers as a whole, but rather bit by bit (in the binary system). Note that bitwise operators have higher precedence than comparison operations.

print(10 > 5 and 10 > 6)
print(10 > 5 & 10 > 6)
print(5 & 10)
print(10 > False > 6)
print((10 > 5) & (10 > 6))

True
False
0
False
True








Tipp





In general, bitwise operators are not needed for data analysis. Avoid unnecessary mistakes: do not use the bitwise operators &, ^, and |.
However, the operators &, ^, and | have a different meaning for sets (which we will cover later). In other modules, these operators also have a different syntactic role, e.g., in the Pandas package when passing multiple slicing conditions: df = df[(condition1) & (condition2) | (condition3)].

title: 2  Data Types
text:
2.6 Boolean Value Exercises
Solve the exercises using Python.

Is the ratio of 44 to 4.5 greater than 10?
Is it true that 4.5 is greater than 4 but less than 5?
Is 2 to the power of 10 equal to 1024?
Are the numbers 3, 4, and 5 divisible by 2 OR not equal to 10?
Check whether a person has to pay the full price based on their age.
Adults pay full price. Children under 14 travel for free. Teenagers from 14 up to 18 years old and seniors from 65 years onwards get a discount.







Tipp 2.2: Sample Solution for Boolean Values







# 1.
print('Is 44/4.5 greater than 10?', 44/4.5 > 10)
# 2.
print('Is 4.5 greater than 4 but less than 5?', 4 < 4.5 and 4.5 < 5)
# 3.
print('Is 2 to the power of 10 equal to 1024?', 2**10 == 1024)
# 4.
print('Are the numbers 3, 4, and 5 divisible by 2 OR not equal to 10?')
print('3:', 3%2 == 0 or 3 != 10)
print('4:', 4%2 == 0 or 4 != 10)
print('5:', 5%2 == 0 or 5 != 10)
# 5. Check age

# Variable for the age to check
age = 17
print('Age =', age)

print('The person has to pay full price:', age >= 18 and age < 65)
print('The person gets a discount:', age >= 14 and age < 18 or age >= 65)
print('The person travels for free:', age < 14)

Is 44/4.5 greater than 10? False
Is 4.5 greater than 4 but less than 5? True
Is 2 to the power of 10 equal to 1024? True
Are the numbers 3, 4, and 5 divisible by 2 OR not equal to 10?
3: True
4: True
5: True
Age = 17
The person has to pay full price: False
The person gets a discount: True
The person travels for free: False


Sample Solution by Marc Sönnecken and Maik Poetzsch

title: 2  Data Types
text:
2.7 Strings
Strings are enclosed in single or double quotes in Python.

print('a string')
print("another string")

a string
another string


Within a string, single or double quotes can be used, as long as they don’t match the quotes surrounding the string.

print('A sophisticated heap beam, which we call a "LASER".')
print("I've turned the moon into what I like to call a 'Death Star'.")

A sophisticated heap beam, which we call a "LASER".
I've turned the moon into what I like to call a 'Death Star'.


The control character \ (also called the escape character) allows you to use certain special characters.

print("The control character \\ allows the same \"quotation marks\" in the output of print.")
print("First a\tTab, then a\nnew line.")

The control character \ allows the same "quotation marks" in the output of print.
First a Tab, then a
new line.


A prefixed r makes the backslash \ treated literally (raw string literal). This is useful, for example, when working with file paths.

print("The data is located at: C:\tolle_daten\nordpol\weihnachtsmann")
print(r"The data is located at: C:\tolle_daten\nordpol\weihnachtsmann")

The data is located at: C:  olle_daten
ordpol\weihnachtsmann
The data is located at: C:\tolle_daten\nordpol\weihnachtsmann

title: 2  Data Types
text:
2.8 String Operations
Some operators also work with data of type string.

# string + string
print('a' + 'b')

# string * number
print(15 * 'a')

# logical operators
print('a' < 'b', 'a' >= 'b', 'a' != 'b')
print('a' or 'b', 'a' and 'b')

ab
aaaaaaaaaaaaaaa
True False True
a b

title: 2  Data Types
text:
2.10 Variables
Variables are placeholders or references to data. Assignment is done using the assignment operator =. A variable name may only consist of letters, numbers, and underscores. The first character cannot be a number.

var_1 = 'ABC'
var_2 = 26
var_3 = True

print("The", var_1, "has", var_2, "letters. This is", var_3)

The ABC has 26 letters. This is True


In Python, variables do not need to be initialized. The data type of a variable is determined by assigning an appropriate value.

print("The variable var_1 has the type", type(var_1))
print("The variable var_2 has the type", type(var_2))
print("The variable var_3 has the type", type(var_3))

The variable var_1 has the type <class 'str'>
The variable var_2 has the type <class 'int'>
The variable var_3 has the type <class 'bool'>


The data type of a variable changes when it is assigned a new value of a different data type.

var_1 = 100
print("The variable var_1 has the type", type(var_1))

The variable var_1 has the type <class 'int'>


Similarly, the data type of a variable can change to accommodate the result of an operation. In this example, the result cannot be stored as an integer. Python therefore assigns the data type ‘float’ to the object var_1.

var_1 = var_1 / 11
print("The variable var_1 has the type", type(var_1))

The variable var_1 has the type <class 'float'>


Python provides functions to determine the data type of a variable and to convert it. The function type() has already been used in the code examples to check the data type (i.e., class) of objects. The following code block demonstrates type conversion.

a = 67
print(a, type(a))

b = a + 1.8
print(b, type(b), "\n")

print(f"Note the truncation of the decimal part:\nConverting to integers with int(): {(a := int(a)), (b := int(b))}\n")

print(f"Converting to ASCII characters with chr(): {(a := chr(a)), (b := chr(b))}\n")

print(f"Converting to an ASCII number with ord(): {(a := ord(a)), (b := ord(b))}\n")

print(f"Converting to floating-point numbers with float(): {(a := float(a)), (b := float(b))}\n")

print(f"Converting to strings with str(): {(a := str(a)), (b := str(b))}\n")

print(f"Converting to Boolean values with bool(): {bool(a), bool(b)}")

67 <class 'int'>
68.8 <class 'float'> 

Note the truncation of the decimal part:
Converting to integers with int(): (67, 68)

Converting to ASCII characters with chr(): ('C', 'D')

Converting to an ASCII number with ord(): (67, 68)

Converting to floating-point numbers with float(): (67.0, 68.0)

Converting to strings with str(): ('67.0', '68.0')

Converting to Boolean values with bool(): (True, True)



Additional Assignment Operators
In the code block above, the so-called Walrus Operator := was used. This allows assignments to be made within an expression (here, inside the print() function). Python has a variety of other assignment operators (see the Python documentation or a more user-friendly overview here).



Operator
Equivalent Assignment




a += 2
a = a + 2


a -= 2
a = a - 2


a *= 2
a = a * 2


a /= 2
a = a / 2


a %= 2
a = a % 2


a //= 2
a = a // 2


a **= 2
a = a ** 2









Tipp 2.4: Readable Assignments





The assignment operators shown in the table are easy to read and understand for someone reviewing your code, as assignments always appear at the start of a line, aligned to the left.
In contrast, the Walrus Operator can appear anywhere in your code. While this can be convenient while writing, it can reduce readability. If you use the Walrus Operator, make sure your code remains understandable.





Naming Variables
There are usually only a few rules for naming variables. Still, it is better to choose a long but descriptive variable name than a short one that is easy to type. Code is read far more often than it is written. Can you remember? What value is stored in the variable Var_3, and what values are stored in variable1 and a? It’s enough if you can recall the correct data type.
If you can’t remember, this is an example of poor naming: these variable names are not descriptive. The solution is in the expandable section below.






Tipp 2.5: Variable Solution






print(var_3, type(var_3))
print(variable1, type(variable1))
print(a, type(a))

True <class 'bool'>
15 <class 'int'>
67.0 <class 'str'>





Therefore, it is recommended to use “descriptive,” i.e., self-explanatory, variable names. Self-explanatory variable names are those where the name describes the content of the variable. For example, if a variable stores a student’s degree, it can be named academic_degree or studienabschluss. If data from different years are being processed, the year can be included in the variable name for better distinction, e.g., academic_degree_2023 or studienabschluss2024. This improves code readability and makes using the variable easier. More information can be found in this Wikipedia section.






Hinweis 2.2: Keywords and Function Names



Reserved keywords and function names in Python are unsuitable as variable names. While Python will raise a syntax error if you try to assign a value to keywords like True or break, function names can be reassigned, for example with print = 6. After doing so, calling the print function will no longer work. In this case, you must remove the assignment from the script and restart Python.
The following keywords exist in Python:
and        continue   for        lambda     try
as         def        from       nonlocal   while
assert     del        global     not        with
async      elif       if         or         yield
await      else       import     pass       True
break      except     in         raise      class
False      finally    is         return     None

title: 2  Data Types
text:
2.11 Variable Exercises

Write a script that converts a given time 𝗍 in seconds into the number of days, hours, minutes, and seconds, and prints this breakdown.
Calculate the breakdown for the following times:

79,222 s
90,061 s
300,000 s

The position of a vehicle at time \(t\), which accelerates constantly with acceleration \(a\), is given by:

\[
x(t) = x_0 + v_0 \cdot t + \frac{1}{2} \cdot a t^2
\]
Here, \(x_0\) is the initial position and \(v_0\) is the initial velocity. Create variables for \(x_0\), \(v_0\), and \(a\), and assign them values. Since the variables only store numerical values without units, consider any necessary unit conversions. You can use the following example values:
\[
x_0 = 10 \, km \,\,\,v_0 = 50 \frac{km}{h} \,\,\,a = 0.1 \frac{m}{s^2}
\]
Create a variable for the time \(t\), e.g.: \(t = 10 \, min\).
Use the above equation and your variables to calculate the position \(x(t)\).
Output not only the value of \(x(t)\) in kilometers, but embed it in a full sentence including units.






Tipp 2.6: Sample Solution for Variable Exercises







Exercise


# 1.
def time_breakdown(t):
    days = t // (24*3600)
    t = t % (24*3600)
    hours = t // 3600
    t = t % 3600
    minutes = t // 60
    seconds = t % 60
    
    return days, hours, minutes, seconds

# Enter the values to check: a, b, c
given_time = 79222

days, hours, minutes, seconds = time_breakdown(given_time)

print(f"{given_time} seconds correspond to {days} days, {hours} hours, {minutes} minutes, and {seconds} seconds")

79222 seconds correspond to 0 days, 22 hours, 0 minutes, and 22 seconds



Exercise


# 2.
# Initial values including conversions
x0 = 10*1000 # kilometers to meters
v0 = 50/3.6 # km/h to m/s
a = 0.1
t = 10*60 # minutes to seconds

x = x0 + v0 * t + 0.5 * a * t **2

print(f"The calculated position of the vehicle at time t = {(t/60):.0f} minutes is {(x/1000):.2f} kilometers.")

The calculated position of the vehicle at time t = 10 minutes is 36.33 kilometers.


Sample Solution by Marc Sönnecken




(Arnold 2023a, 2023b)




Arnold, Simone. 2023a. „Datenanalyse mit Python. Datentypen und Grundlagen.“ Fachhochschule Dortmund.


———. 2023b. „Datenanalyse mit Python. Funktionen Module Dateien.“ Fachhochschule Dortmund.

title: 3  Functions: Basics
text:
3.1 Functions and Methods
Functions are subroutines that bundle program instructions so that parts of a program can be reused multiple times. This allows a program to be written more quickly and makes it easier to read. As you can see in the documentation, Python comes with a manageable number of basic functions. This chapter teaches the general usage of functions included in Python.
Python is continuously evolving: new versions with new features are released regularly. Therefore, this chapter also provides a number of tips on how to read Python’s documentation. This is especially relevant when extending Python extensively with modules. For example, functions in the Pandas module often have dozens of documented parameters.
In Python, there are two types of callable objects: functions and methods.

title: 3  Functions: Basics
text:
Functions
Functions can take objects of any data type as input.
Functions are called using their function name followed by parentheses (). An example is the print() function:

var_str = 'ABC'
var_int = 26
var_bool = True

print("The variable var_str has type", type(var_str))
print("The variable var_int has type", type(var_int))
print("The variable var_bool has type", type(var_bool))

The variable var_str has type <class 'str'>
The variable var_int has type <class 'int'>
The variable var_bool has type <class 'bool'>


Functions must always return a value. If functions cannot or should not return a value, the value None is returned, which indicates a non-existent value.

res = print(15)
print(res)

15
None


Functions can be nested and thus executed from the inside out in sequence. In this code example, first the sum of two numbers is calculated, and then the boolean value of the result is determined. This value is then output using the print function.

print(bool(sum([1, 2])))

True




Methods
Methods are a feature of object-oriented programming languages. In the previous chapter, it was explained that in Python, objects belong to a specific type or class, and depending on the values stored within them, they have an appropriate data type. Methods are functions that belong to a specific class and are only available for objects of that class. Methods can also be defined for multiple classes. Methods are called by appending a dot . to the object followed by the method name: variable.method or (value).method. For example, .upper(), .lower(), and .title() are methods defined for strings.

awesome_text = "Python 3.12 is awesome."

print(awesome_text.upper())
print(awesome_text.lower())
print(awesome_text.title(), "\n")

print(("It also works with values placed in parentheses.").upper())

PYTHON 3.12 IS AWESOME.
python 3.12 is awesome.
Python 3.12 Is Awesome. 

IT ALSO WORKS WITH VALUES PLACED IN PARENTHESES.


Methods like .lower() are not available for objects with an inappropriate data type.

print((1).upper())



'int' object has no attribute 'upper'


Methods can be chained and executed one after another. In this example, the string ‘Cat’ is converted to lowercase, and then the frequency of the letter ‘c’ is counted.

print('Cat'.lower().count('c'))

1


Which methods are available for an object can be determined using the function dir(object). However, the output of this function is often very extensive. To select the relevant entries, the output needs to be filtered. This is not strictly necessary—interested users can refer to Beispiel 3.1.






Beispiel 3.1: Determining an Object’s Methods





The function dir(object) can be used to display the methods available for an object. However, it also shows the attributes and methods of the object’s class, so the output is often very extensive. For example, for the integer 1:

print(dir(1))

['__abs__', '__add__', '__and__', '__bool__', '__ceil__', '__class__', '__delattr__', '__dir__', '__divmod__', '__doc__', '__eq__', '__float__', '__floor__', '__floordiv__', '__format__', '__ge__', '__getattribute__', '__getnewargs__', '__getstate__', '__gt__', '__hash__', '__index__', '__init__', '__init_subclass__', '__int__', '__invert__', '__le__', '__lshift__', '__lt__', '__mod__', '__mul__', '__ne__', '__neg__', '__new__', '__or__', '__pos__', '__pow__', '__radd__', '__rand__', '__rdivmod__', '__reduce__', '__reduce_ex__', '__repr__', '__rfloordiv__', '__rlshift__', '__rmod__', '__rmul__', '__ror__', '__round__', '__rpow__', '__rrshift__', '__rshift__', '__rsub__', '__rtruediv__', '__rxor__', '__setattr__', '__sizeof__', '__str__', '__sub__', '__subclasshook__', '__truediv__', '__trunc__', '__xor__', 'as_integer_ratio', 'bit_count', 'bit_length', 'conjugate', 'denominator', 'from_bytes', 'imag', 'is_integer', 'numerator', 'real', 'to_bytes']


To limit the output to methods, the following list comprehension can be used:

object_ = 1

methods = [attr for attr in dir(object_) if callable(getattr(object_, attr))]
print(methods)

['__abs__', '__add__', '__and__', '__bool__', '__ceil__', '__class__', '__delattr__', '__dir__', '__divmod__', '__eq__', '__float__', '__floor__', '__floordiv__', '__format__', '__ge__', '__getattribute__', '__getnewargs__', '__getstate__', '__gt__', '__hash__', '__index__', '__init__', '__init_subclass__', '__int__', '__invert__', '__le__', '__lshift__', '__lt__', '__mod__', '__mul__', '__ne__', '__neg__', '__new__', '__or__', '__pos__', '__pow__', '__radd__', '__rand__', '__rdivmod__', '__reduce__', '__reduce_ex__', '__repr__', '__rfloordiv__', '__rlshift__', '__rmod__', '__rmul__', '__ror__', '__round__', '__rpow__', '__rrshift__', '__rshift__', '__rsub__', '__rtruediv__', '__rxor__', '__setattr__', '__sizeof__', '__str__', '__sub__', '__subclasshook__', '__truediv__', '__trunc__', '__xor__', 'as_integer_ratio', 'bit_count', 'bit_length', 'conjugate', 'from_bytes', 'is_integer', 'to_bytes']


Methods enclosed with double underscores are defined for the class. The following function removes methods with double underscores from the output:

object_instance = 1

attributes = [attr for attr in dir(object_instance) if (callable(getattr(object_instance, attr)) and not attr.startswith('__'))]
print(attributes)

['as_integer_ratio', 'bit_count', 'bit_length', 'conjugate', 'from_bytes', 'is_integer', 'to_bytes']


In the case of an integer, methods (to distinguish from floating-point numbers in enclosing parentheses) can be called as follows:

(1).as_integer_ratio()

(1, 1)


Methods of the object ‘awesome_text’:

object = awesome_text

attributes = [attr for attr in dir(object) if (callable(getattr(object, attr)) and not attr.startswith('__'))]
print(attributes)

['capitalize', 'casefold', 'center', 'count', 'encode', 'endswith', 'expandtabs', 'find', 'format', 'format_map', 'index', 'isalnum', 'isalpha', 'isascii', 'isdecimal', 'isdigit', 'isidentifier', 'islower', 'isnumeric', 'isprintable', 'isspace', 'istitle', 'isupper', 'join', 'ljust', 'lower', 'lstrip', 'maketrans', 'partition', 'removeprefix', 'removesuffix', 'replace', 'rfind', 'rindex', 'rjust', 'rpartition', 'rsplit', 'rstrip', 'split', 'splitlines', 'startswith', 'strip', 'swapcase', 'title', 'translate', 'upper', 'zfill']

title: 3  Functions: Basics
text:
3.2 Parameters
Many functions and methods can accept multiple parameters, separated by commas. The values passed to a function are called arguments (Python Documentation). Parameters control the execution of a program. The parameters available for the print() function are listed in the function documentation:
print(*objects, sep=' ', end='\n', file=None, flush=False)
*objects, sep, end, file, and flush are the parameters of the print() function.

Parameters without an equal sign = must be provided when calling the function or method. Parameters with an equal sign = are optional and can be provided during the call.
The values after the equal sign indicate the default values of the parameters. These are used if an argument is not explicitly passed during the call.







Tipp 3.2: Exceptions for Default Values





The values listed in a function’s definition are not always the actual default values. Therefore, it is recommended to read the parameter descriptions when using a function.
Some functions use the keyword None to indicate the default value. The value None serves as a placeholder. An example is the NumPy function numpy.loadtxt().
numpy.loadtxt(fname, dtype=<class 'float'>, comments='#', delimiter=None, converters=None, /
              skiprows=0, usecols=None, unpack=False, ndmin=0, encoding=None, max_rows=None, /
              *, quotechar=None, like=None)

For the parameter delimiter, the default value is listed as the keyword None. However, according to the function description, the actual default is whitespace: “The default is whitespace.”
The parameter usecols also has the default value None: “The default, None, results in all columns being read.”

Another example is the function pandas.read_csv(). Some arguments have the default value <no_default>. (Only selected parameters are shown below).
pandas.read_csv(sep=<no_default>, verbose=<no_default>)
From the description, the actual default values can be seen:
sep : str, default ‘,’
verbose : bool, default False




In Python, arguments can either be passed as a positional argument. This means Python expects arguments in a fixed order corresponding to the parameters in the function definition. Alternatively, arguments can be passed as keyword arguments, where inputs are mapped according to the parameter name. By default, arguments can be passed either positionally or by keyword. Deviations from this are indicated by the symbols * and / (see the tip below).







Tipp 3.3: Positional and Keyword Arguments, *args and **kwargs





The symbols * and / indicate which parameters can or must be passed positionally or as keyword arguments.




Left side
Separator
Right side




positional-only arguments
/
positional or keyword arguments


positional or keyword arguments
*
keyword-only arguments



(https://realpython.com/python-asterisk-and-slash-special-parameters/)

 
An example of the * separator is the glob function from the module of the same name. The pathname parameter can be passed positionally (first position) or as a keyword. The other parameters must be passed as keyword arguments.
glob.glob(pathname, *, root_dir=None, dir_fd=None, recursive=False, include_hidden=False)
Both control characters can appear within a function definition, but only in the order / and *. In the reverse order, it would be impossible to pass arguments. An example is the sorted function. The first parameter must be passed positionally, while the key and reverse parameters must be passed as keywords.
sorted(iterable, /, *, key=None, reverse=False)¶






WarnungExceptions



Some functions deviate from the usual pattern, for example the functions min() and max(). They are defined (among others) in the following form:
min(iterable, *, key=None)
max(iterable, *, key=None)
Both functions accept the iterable parameter, but not as a keyword.


Many functions can accept an arbitrary number of arguments, either positionally or as keywords. This is generally indicated by the keywords *args (positional arguments) and **kwargs (keyword arguments). The difference is marked by the single or double asterisk, and the keywords themselves can be replaced (as with the function print(*objects)). The keyword *args also corresponds to the * symbol in the function definition, meaning that only keyword arguments can follow it. More information can be found here.



In the function definition of print(), *objects is a positional parameter (always in the first position), has no default value, and can accept an arbitrary number of arguments (n inputs are placed in the first n positions). The other parameters of the print() function are optional and must be passed as keyword arguments.

title: 3  Functions: Basics
text:
3.3 Function Exercises

True or False: Methods are available depending on the data type of a value or an object.
Print the three values 1, 2, and 3 using print(). Parameterize the function so that the output looks like this:



1_x_2_x_3



Look up the function bool() in the documentation.


Which parameters does the function accept, and which of them are optional?
Which arguments are passed positionally and which can be passed as keyword arguments? Is the method of passing fixed or flexible?







TippSolutions





Exercise 1: correct
Exercise 2

print(1, 2, 3, sep="_x_")

Task 3: The function bool() has an optional argument object with a default value of False. The argument must be passed positionally.

title: 4  Control Flow
text:
Branching
Control flow allows you to manage the execution of parts of a program. Statements can be skipped or executed multiple times.
Branching allows for conditional execution, where different parts of the script run depending on one or more conditions.
In Python, branching starts with the keyword if. This is followed by the branch condition and ends with a colon :. If the branch condition is true, the indented block of statements is executed.
# Example: Number smaller than a threshold

a = 7
if a < 10:
    print('The number', a, 'is smaller than 10.')

The number 7 is smaller than 10.
It is also possible to define an alternative block of statements that will be executed if the condition is false. This is implemented using the else keyword.
# Example: Number below a threshold with alternative output

a = 13
if a < 10:
    print('The number', a, 'is less than 10.')
else:
    print('The number', a, 'is not less than 10.')

The number 13 is not less than 10.
Multiple conditions can also be used.
# Example: Number within the range between 10 and 20

num = 1
if num < 20 and num > 10:
    print('The number', num, 'is between 10 and 20.')
else:
    print('The number', num, 'is not between 10 and 20.')

The number 1 is not between 10 and 20.
Finally, several alternative conditions can be checked. This is possible by nesting branches.
# Example: Number within the range between 10 and 20 using nested conditions

number = 12
if number > 10:
    print('The number', number, 'is greater than 10.')
    
    if number < 20:
        print('The number', number, 'is less than 20.')
        print('Therefore, the number is between 10 and 20.')
    else:
        print('The number', number, 'is greater than 20 and not within the desired range.')
else:
    print('The number', number, 'is less than 10 and not within the desired range.')

The number 12 is greater than 10.
The number 12 is less than 20.
Therefore, the number is between 10 and 20.
On the other hand, this is possible with the keyword elif.
# Example: Number in the range between 10 and 20 using elif

number = 112
if number < 20 and number > 10:
    print('The number', number, 'is between 10 and 20.')
elif number < 10:
    print('The number', number, 'is less than 10 and not in the desired range.')
elif number > 20 and number <= 100:
    print('The number', number, 'is greater than 20, but not greater than 100.')
elif number > 20 and number <= 1000:
    print('The number', number, 'is greater than 20, but not greater than 1000.')
else:
    print('The number', number, 'is not between 10 and 20 and is greater than 1000.')

The number 112 is greater than 20, but not greater than 1000.

title: 4  Control Flow
text:
4.1 Control Flow Exercises

Write a program that counts from 1 to 25 and from 38 to 50, and prints every value that is divisible by 7 using print().
Roulette: Write a program that checks whether a given number is within the valid range of the roulette table. If not, it should display an error message. If it is, the program should output whether the number is:


even or odd,
red, black, or green,
low (1-18) or high (19-36), and
in the 1st, 2nd, or 3rd dozen.





Roulette Table


European roulette by Betzaar.com is licensed under CC 3.0 BY-SA and available on wikimedia.org. 2010

 






TippSample Solution for Control Flow Exercises







Exercise


# Write a program that counts from 1 to 25 and from 38 to 50, printing each value divisible by 7 using print()

i = 0
while i < 50:
    i += 1
    if i > 25 and i < 38 or i % 7 != 0:
        continue
    else:
        print(i)

# Alternative using lists
# Note: as mentioned in the "Loops" section, the stop value of the range function must be one higher than the last desired number, because Python counts exclusively
numbers = list(range(1,26)) + list(range(38,51))
for i in numbers:
    if i % 7 == 0:
        print(i)

7
14
21
42
49
7
14
21
42
49



Exercise


# Define the integer to check as a variable
x = 10

# The red and black numbers are defined as lists (a tuple would also work here)
red_numbers = [1,3,5,7,9,12,14,16,18,21,23,25,27,28,30,32,34,36]
black_numbers = [2,4,6,8,10,11,13,15,17,19,20,22,24,26,29,31,33,35]

# The first if-statement checks whether the number is within the valid range. If not, it jumps directly to the "else" branch below
if x >= 0 and x <= 36:

    # Inside this if-statement, there are further if-statements that check other characteristics:

    # Check whether the number is even or odd
    if x % 2 == 0:
        print("Number is even")
    else:
        print("Number is odd")

    # Check whether the number is red, black, or green
    if x in red_numbers:
        print("Number is red")
    elif x in black_numbers:
        print("Number is black")
    elif x == 0:
        print("Number is green")

    # Check whether the number is high or low
    if x >= 1 and x <= 18:
        print("Number is low")
    elif x >= 19 and x <= 36:
        print("Number is high")
    
    # Check which dozen the number belongs to
    if x >= 1 and x <= 12:
        print("Number is in the 1st dozen")
    elif x >= 13 and x <= 24:
        print("Number is in the 2nd dozen")
    elif x >= 25 and x <= 36:
        print("Number is in the 3rd dozen")
    else:
        print("Number is not in any dozen")
    
else:
    print("Number is not within the valid range.")

Number is even
Number is black
Number is low
Number is in the 1st dozen


Sample solution by Marc Sönnecken and Maik Poetzsch




 
(Arnold (2023))




Arnold, Simone. 2023. „Datenanalyse mit Python. Schleifen und Abzweigungen.“ Fachhochschule Dortmund.


Matthes, Eric. 2017. Python Crashkurs: Eine praktische, projektbasierte Programmiereinführung. 1. Aufl. Heidelberg: dpunkt.verlag.

title: 5  Collection Types
text:
5.1 Lists
Collection types are used to store and manage multiple values in a single variable. In Python, there are four collection types, each represented by its own class:
In this chapter, the four collection types are briefly introduced first. Then, we will focus on how to work with lists in particular.
Like all types in Python, lists are created by assignment. When defining a list, elements are enclosed in square brackets [] and separated by commas ,. Lists can be concatenated with the + operator. * repeats a list n times.
text_variable = 'abc'

list1 = [1, 'xy', True, text_variable]
print(list1)

# Lists can also contain other lists
list2 = [None, list1]
print(list2)

# Lists can be concatenated with + and repeated with *
print(list1 + list2)
print(list1 * 2)

[1, 'xy', True, 'abc']
[None, [1, 'xy', True, 'abc']]
[1, 'xy', True, 'abc', None, [1, 'xy', True, 'abc']]
[1, 'xy', True, 'abc', 1, 'xy', True, 'abc']
An empty list can be created by assigning [].
empty_list = []
print(empty_list)

[]

title: 5  Collection Types
text:
Slicing: the Access Operator []
Accessing individual or multiple elements of a list (and other collection types) is done using the access operator []. A portion of an object is called a slice, which is why the operator is also called the slice operator.

Accessing Individual Elements
Elements are accessed by their index, starting at 0.

print(list1)
print(list1[0])
print(list1[3])

[1, 'xy', True, 'abc']
1
abc


Nested lists can be accessed using two consecutive indexing operators. The list list2 contains, at index position 1, a list with 4 elements.

print(list2)
print(list2[1])
print(list2[1][0], list2[1][1], list2[1][2], list2[1][3])

[None, [1, 'xy', True, 'abc']]
[1, 'xy', True, 'abc']
1 xy True abc


With negative indices, elements can be accessed from the end of a list. For example, -1 refers to the last element.

print(list1)
print(list1[-1], list1[-3])

[1, 'xy', True, 'abc']
abc xy




Accessing Multiple Elements
Index ranges can be accessed using the [start:stop:step] syntax. start is the first element to be included, stop is the first element not included, and step defines the increment.




Access Operator
Slice




list[start:stop]
Elements from start to stop - 1


list[:]
All elements of the list


list[start:]
Elements from start to the end of the list


list[:stop]
Elements from the beginning of the list up to stop - 1


list[::3]
Every third element



 
Negative values for start, stop, or step select elements in reverse order.



Access Operator
Slice




list[-1]
the last element of the list


list[-2:]
the last two elements of the list


list[:-2]
all elements except the last two


list[::-1]
all elements in reverse order


list[1::-1]
the first two elements in reverse order


list[:-3:-1]
the last two elements in reverse order


list[-3::-1]
all elements except the last two in reverse order



 
(Examples by Greg Hewgill, licensed under CC BY-SA 4.0, available on stackoverflow, 2009)



Strings
Slices can also be applied to strings.

print('Ich bin ein string'[::2])
print('Hallo Welt'[0:6])
print('abc'[::-1])

Ihbnensrn
Hallo 
cba





List Methods
Various methods are defined for the list type.

Determining Elements

list.index(x, start, stop) returns the index position of the first element x. The optional arguments start and stop allow you to limit the search range.
list.count(x) returns the frequency of x in the list.
list.reverse() reverses the order of the list elements (this modifies the list!).
list.sort(reverse = False) sorts the list, with the optional argument reverse = True for descending order (this modifies the list!). The data types within the list must be sortable (i.e., all elements are numeric or strings).


print(list1)

list1.reverse()
print(list1)

# True is counted as 1
print("True is counted as 1:", list1.index(1), list1.count(1))

[1, 'xy', True, 'abc']
['abc', True, 'xy', 1]
True is counted as 1: 1 2




Inserting Elements

list.append(x) adds a single element to the end of the list.
list.extend(iterable) adds all elements from the given iterable to the end of the list. The iterable can be a list, a tuple, a set, or a dictionary.
list.insert(i, x) inserts element x at position i.


print(list1, "\n")

list1.append('Hello')
list1.extend(['Hello', 'World!'])
list1.insert(2, '12345')

print(list1)

['abc', True, 'xy', 1] 

['abc', True, '12345', 'xy', 1, 'Hello', 'Hello', 'World!']




Removing Elements

list.remove(x) removes the first occurrence of x in the list and raises a ValueError if x is not in the list.
list.pop(i) removes the element at index position i. If no index is specified, the last element is removed. The method list.pop(i) returns the removed element.
list.clear() removes all elements from a list.


list1.remove('Hello')
print(list1)

list1.pop(2)

['abc', True, '12345', 'xy', 1, 'Hello', 'World!']


'12345'




Copying Lists and List Elements
In Python, lists do not store data directly; instead, they consist of references (pointers) to the memory locations of the elements they contain. When a list is created by assigning it to another list, the elements themselves are not copied; instead, both lists refer to the same memory locations.

# Copying by assignment
list1 = [1, 'xy', True, text_variable]
print("list1:", list1, "\n")
list2 = list1

## Changing an element in list2
list2[0] = 'ABC'
print("list1 also changes because of the assignment to list2:", list1, "\n")

list1: [1, 'xy', True, 'abc'] 

list1 also changes because of the assignment to list2: ['ABC', 'xy', True, 'abc'] 



To copy a list and create a new object in memory, the method list.copy() can be used. Using the slice operator [:] also creates a new list in memory.

# Using the list.copy() method
list1 = [1, 'xy', True, text_variable]
list2 = list1.copy()

# Modify an element in list2
list2[0] = 'ABC'
print("list1 remains unchanged by the assignment to list2:", list1, "\n")

# Using the slice operator
list1 = [1, 'xy', True, text_variable]
list2 = list1[:]

# Modify an element in list2
list2[0] = 'ABC'
print("list1 remains unchanged by the assignment to list2:", list1)

list1 remains unchanged by the assignment to list2: [1, 'xy', True, 'abc'] 

list1 remains unchanged by the assignment to list2: [1, 'xy', True, 'abc']


Copying list items is not a problem in this regard.

# Using the slice operator
list1 = [1, 'xy', True, text_variable]
list2 = list1[0:2]

# Changing an element in list2
list2[0] = 'ABC'
print("list1 remains unchanged by the assignment in list2:", list1)

list1 remains unchanged by the assignment in list2: [1, 'xy', True, 'abc']


To check whether two objects share the same memory space, you can compare their object IDs using the id() function or use the is / is not operators, which internally call id().

list1 = [1, 'xy', True, text_variable]
list2 = list1

print("ID of list1:", id(list1))
print("ID of list2:", id(list2))
print("Is ID of list1 equal to ID of list2:", list1 is list2)

ID of list1: 1498764172096
ID of list2: 1498764172096
Is ID of list1 equal to ID of list2: True








Hinweis 5.1: Identity vs. Value Equality





The is operator checks the identity of two objects and differs from the logical operator ==, which checks for value equality. Since list1 and list2 contain the same elements, both value equality and identity are relevant. The difference between identity and value equality can be illustrated with a single value (the code example suppresses a syntax warning).

# Value equality
print(1 == 1.0)
print(list1 == list2, "\n")

# Identity
print(1 is 1.0)
print(list1 is list2)

True
True 

False
True

title: 5  Collection Types
text:
5.2 Tuples
Tuples are very similar to lists, but tuples are immutable data objects. This means that the elements of an existing tuple cannot be changed or removed. You also cannot add new elements to a tuple.
Tuples are created using parentheses (), and elements are separated by commas ,. A tuple with a single value is created with a comma in the form (value, ). Accessing elements of a tuple is possible using the slice operator [start:stop:step]. Tuples can be concatenated using the + and * operators.

tuple1 = (2, 7.8, 'Fire', True, text_variable)
tuple2 = (1, )

print(tuple1)
print(tuple1[2:4])
print(tuple1[::2])
print(tuple1[-1])
print(tuple1[2:4] + tuple2)
print(3 * tuple2)

(2, 7.8, 'Fire', True, 'abc')
('Fire', True)
(2, 'Fire', 'abc')
abc
('Fire', True, 1)
(1, 1, 1)



Copying Tuples
Tuples behave differently from lists when it comes to copying. Tuples do not have a .copy() method. Using slicing [:] allows you to create a copy, but both tuples initially reference the same memory. When a tuple is reassigned, Python, as with any object, creates a new object in memory.

# Copying by assignment
tuple1 = (1, 2, 3)
tuple2 = tuple1

## Reassigning values of tuple1
tuple1 = (4, 5, 6)
print(f"The values stored in tuple2 remain unchanged:\n{tuple1} {tuple2}\n")

# Copying using slice operator
tuple1 = (1, 2, 3)
tuple2 = tuple1[:]
print(tuple2 is tuple1)

## Reassigning values of tuple1
tuple1 = (4, 5, 6)
print(tuple1, tuple2)

The values stored in tuple2 remain unchanged:
(4, 5, 6) (1, 2, 3)

True
(4, 5, 6) (1, 2, 3)

title: 5  Collection Types
text:
5.3 Sets
In Python, sets can be created from a list or a tuple using the set() function, or directly with curly braces {} (an empty set can only be created with set(), because {} creates an empty dictionary). Sets are unordered collections, so their elements do not have a specific order.

my_list = [1, 1, 5, 3, 3, 4, 2, 'a', 123, 1000, ('tuple', 5)]
print("The list object as a set:\n", set(my_list))

my_set = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 1000, ('tuple', 5), 'a', 123}
print("A set can also be created using curly braces:", my_set, sep="\n")

The list object as a set:
 {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 1000, 'a', ('tuple', 5), 123}
A set can also be created using curly braces:
{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 1000, ('tuple', 5), 'a', 123}


Sets can be used, for example, for comparison operations.

set_a = set('Python')
set_b = set('is awesome')

# unique characters in a
print("Set a:", set_a)

# characters in a but not in b
print("Set a - b:", set_a - set_b)

# characters in a or b
print("Set a | b:", set_a | set_b)

# characters in a and b
print("Set a & b:", set_a & set_b)

# characters in a or b, but not in both (XOR)
print("Set a ^ b:", set_a ^ set_b)

Set a: {'t', 'y', 'P', 'n', 'o', 'h'}
Set a - b: {'t', 'P', 'y', 'n', 'h'}
Set a | b: {'t', 'm', ' ', 'P', 'y', 'w', 's', 'e', 'n', 'o', 'h', 'a', 'i'}
Set a & b: {'o'}
Set a ^ b: {'m', 't', ' ', 'y', 'P', 'w', 's', 'e', 'n', 'a', 'h', 'i'}



Copying Sets
Sets behave similarly to tuples, with the difference that the .copy() method is defined for sets. However, the indexing operator [] cannot be used on sets.

# Copying by assignment
set1 = {1, 2, 3}
set2 = set1
print(set1 is set2)

## Reassigning set1
set1 = {4, 5, 6}
print(f"The values stored in set2 remain unchanged:\n{set1} {set2}")

# Copying using the .copy() method
set1 = {1, 2, 3}
set2 = set1.copy()
print(set1 is set2)

## Reassigning set1
set1 = {4, 5, 6}
print(f"The values stored in set2 remain unchanged:\n{set1} {set2}")

True
The values stored in set2 remain unchanged:
{4, 5, 6} {1, 2, 3}
False
The values stored in set2 remain unchanged:
{4, 5, 6} {1, 2, 3}

title: 5  Collection Types
text:
5.4 Dictionaries
Dictionaries consist of key-value pairs. Keys can be numbers or strings, and each key can only appear once. Dictionaries are defined using curly braces {}. Keys and their corresponding values are separated by a colon :. Values are accessed using the bracket operator [], which contains the key(s). Accessing values by the positional index of keys is not possible, since numbers are interpreted as keys.

dictionary1 = {1: 'abc', 'b': [1, 2, 3], 'c': ('tuple', 5, 6)}
print(dictionary1, "\n")

print("Values of key 1:", dictionary1[1])
print("Values of key 'b':", dictionary1['b'])

{1: 'abc', 'b': [1, 2, 3], 'c': ('tuple', 5, 6)} 

Values of key 1: abc
Values of key 'b': [1, 2, 3]


You can access the keys of a dictionary using the dictionary.keys() method, and the values using the dictionary.values() method.

print("Keys:", dictionary1.keys(), "\n")
print("Values:", dictionary1.values())

Keys: dict_keys([1, 'b', 'c']) 

Values: dict_values(['abc', [1, 2, 3], ('tuple', 5, 6)])



Copying Dictionaries
Dictionaries behave like lists when copied, meaning that copying via assignment will share the same memory space between dictionaries.

# Copying via assignment
print("dictionary:", dictionary1, "\n")
dictionary2 = dictionary1

## Modifying an element in dictionary2
dictionary2[1] = 'ABC'
print("dictionary1 also changes due to the assignment to dictionary2:\n",
       dictionary1, "\n")

# Using the dictionary.copy() method
dictionary1 = {1: 'abc', 'b': [1, 2, 3], 'c': ('tuple', 5, 6)}
dictionary2 = dictionary1.copy()

## Modifying an element in dictionary2
dictionary2[1] = 'ABC'
print("dictionary1 remains unchanged by the assignment to dictionary2:\n",
       dictionary1, "\n")

dictionary: {1: 'abc', 'b': [1, 2, 3], 'c': ('tuple', 5, 6)} 

dictionary1 also changes due to the assignment to dictionary2:
 {1: 'ABC', 'b': [1, 2, 3], 'c': ('tuple', 5, 6)} 

dictionary1 remains unchanged by the assignment to dictionary2:
 {1: 'abc', 'b': [1, 2, 3], 'c': ('tuple', 5, 6)}

title: 5  Collection Types
text:
5.6 Deleting: the del Keyword
To delete collections, elements, or slices, the del keyword can be used.

# Deleting a list
del list1

# Deleting a range of indices from a list
print("List before deletion:", list2)
del list2[1:3]
print("List after deletion:", list2)

# Deleting a key from a dictionary
print("Dictionary before deletion:", dictionary1)
del dictionary1[1]
print("Dictionary after deletion:", dictionary1)

List before deletion: [1, 'xy', True, 'abc']
List after deletion: [1, 'abc']
Dictionary before deletion: {1: 'abc', 'b': [1, 2, 3], 'c': ('tuple', 5, 6)}
Dictionary after deletion: {'b': [1, 2, 3], 'c': ('tuple', 5, 6)}

title: 5  Collection Types
text:
5.7 Functions
Collection types can be converted into each other.

dictionary = {1: 'Tomcat', 2: 'Vixen', 3: 'Doe'}
print( (list_var := list(dictionary)) )
print( (set_var := set(list_var)) )
print( (tuple_var := tuple(set_var)) )

[1, 2, 3]
{1, 2, 3}
(1, 2, 3)


Some practical functions can also be applied to collection types:

len() returns the number of elements in a collection.
min(), max(), sum() return the minimum, maximum, or sum of a collection (for dictionaries, the number of keys is counted).

title: 5  Collection Types
text:
5.8 Operations: Using Loops
To apply arithmetic and logical operators to the elements stored in a collection, a for-loop is used. In the following example, a list numbers is iterated over, the numbers it contains are squared, and each result is appended to the list squared_numbers. It also checks whether the squared numbers are divisible by 3 and stores the check results in the list modulo_3.




numbers = list(range(1, 11))

squared_numbers = [] # the list must be created before the loop
modulo_3 = [] # create an empty list before the loop

for number in numbers:
  squared_number = number ** 2
  squared_numbers.append(squared_number)
  modulo_3.append(squared_number % 3 == 0)

print(squared_numbers)
print(modulo_3)

[1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49, 64, 81, 100]
[False, False, True, False, False, True, False, False, True, False]

5.9 Collection Types Exercises

Speed Conversion
Create a script that generates a conversion table for speeds. The following conditions should be observed:


The conversion should be from km/h to m/s.
The start and end values should be freely selectable in km/h, with both being integers.
The number of conversion points should be definable, and the intermediate steps (in km/h) should always be output as whole numbers.

Tip: In your script, you can use the input() function to capture values from the user.

Sorting: Given the list my_list = list(range(9, 0, -1)). It should be sorted using for-loops (for example, using the Bubble Sort algorithm).







Tipp 5.2: Sample Solution for Collection Types Exercises






Square numbers divisible by 3


numbers = list(range(1, 11))

square_numbers = [] # the list must be created before the loop

print(square_numbers)

[9, 36, 81]



Conversion of Speeds


# Free input
## start = int(input("Enter starting value in kilometers per hour: "))
## end = int(input("Enter ending value in kilometers per hour: "))
## steps = int(input("Enter number of steps to output: "))

# Fixed values for the solution
start = 5

# Conversion

# Output

Sorting: Bubble Sort Algorithm


# static list, text output
my_list = list(range(9, 0, -1))

if len(my_list) > 1: 

    print("Initial list\t\t  :", my_list)

    # outer loop
    step = 0
    for i in range(len(my_list) - 1):

        # inner loop
        for j in range(len(my_list) - 1):
            if my_list[j] > my_list[j + 1]:
                my_list[j], my_list[j + 1] = my_list[j + 1], my_list[j]

        step += 1
        print("List after step", step, ":", my_list)

    print("\nSorted list:", *my_list) # * suppresses commas between list elements

else:
    print("The list must contain at least two elements!")

title: 6  Defining Your Own Functions
text:
6.1 Syntax
Defining your own functions opens up a wide range of possibilities in Python:
The keyword def starts the function definition. It is followed by the function name and the function parameters, which are enclosed in parentheses (). The function header ends with a colon :. The function’s code block is indented. Every function returns a value, which is passed back to the calling location using the return keyword. return ends the function’s execution, even if it is not at the end of the code block.
def function_name(parameter1, parameter2):
    code_block
    return return_value
In order for the function to be executed, the defined function name must be called. The function contains another statement after the keyword return, which is no longer executed.
# Example 1: Sum of Squares

# Definition of a function to calculate the sum of squares of two arguments
def sum_squares(a, b):
    print('Argument a:', a)
    print('Argument b:', b)
    print(18 * '=')
    total = a**2 + b**2
    return total
    print("Statements after the 'return' keyword are no longer executed.")

print(sum_squares(6, 7))

Argument a: 6
Argument b: 7
==================
85
The return value can be stored in a variable.
result = sum_squares(6, 7)
print(result)

Argument a: 6
Argument b: 7
==================
85

title: 6  Defining Your Own Functions
text:
6.2 Optional Parameters
Optional parameters can be used to control the execution of a program. Optional parameters must be defined after the required parameters. In this example, the print output of the function is controlled using the parameter output.

# Example 2: optional arguments

# Define a function to calculate the sum of the squares of two arguments
def sum_squares(a, b, output=False):
    if output:
        print('Value of argument a:', a)
        print('Value of argument b:', b)
        print(18 * '=')
    total = a**2 + b**2
    return total

print(sum_squares(42, 7), "\n")
print(sum_squares(42, 7, output=True))

1813 

Value of argument a: 42
Value of argument b: 7
==================
1813


If there are multiple optional parameters, arguments are assigned positionally or using the keyword.

# Example 3: multiple optional arguments

# Define a function to calculate the sum of powers of two arguments
def sum_powers(a, b, p=2, output=False):
    if output:
        print('Argument a:', a)
        print('Argument b:', b)
        print('Argument p:', p)
        print(18 * '=')
    total = a**p + b**p
    return total

# positional passing
print(sum_powers(42, 7, 3, True), "\n")

# keyword passing
print(sum_powers(42, 7, output=True, p=4))

Argument a: 42
Argument b: 7
Argument p: 3
==================
74431 

Argument a: 42
Argument b: 7
Argument p: 4
==================
3114097

title: 6  Defining Your Own Functions
text:
6.3 Return Value(s)
In Python, functions can only have a single return value. However, multiple values can be separated by commas. Python returns these as a tuple.

# Example 4: multiple return values

# Definition of a function to calculate the sum of powers of two arguments
def sum_powers(a, b, p = 2, output = False):
    if output:
        print('Argument a:', a)
        print('Argument b:', b)
        print('Argument p:', p)
        print(18 * '=')
    total = a**p + b**p
    return a, b, total

result = sum_powers(2, 7, output = False, p = 4)
print(result, type(result))

(2, 7, 2417) <class 'tuple'>


The slice operator allows you to select a specific return value.

print(result[2])

sum_of_powers = sum_powers(2, 7, output=False, p=4)[2]
print(sum_of_powers, type(sum_of_powers))

2417
2417 <class 'int'>

title: 6  Defining Your Own Functions
text:
6.4 Exercises Defining Functions

Palindrome
Write a function is_palindrome() that checks whether a given string is a palindrome.

Note: A palindrome is a string that reads the same forwards and backwards, such as ‘Anna’, ‘Otto’, ‘racecar’. Palindromes do not have to consist only of letters; they can also contain numbers or a combination of letters and numbers, like ‘345g543’.

Fibonacci Sequence

Develop a function fibonacci(n) that generates the first n numbers of the Fibonacci sequence and returns them as a list. The Fibonacci sequence starts with 0 and 1, and each subsequent number is the sum of the two preceding numbers.

Encryption

In geocaching, encrypted messages are often used as puzzles. One common encryption logic is:

A -> Z
B -> Y
C -> X
…

Write a function encrypt(string) that takes a string as input and returns an encrypted string. How can you most easily decrypt an encrypted string?

Temperature Converter

Develop a function convert_temperature(value, from_unit, to_unit) that converts a temperature from one unit to another. The function should use the following parameters:

value: The temperature value to be converted.
from_unit / to_unit: The unit of the input and target value as a string. Possible values are ‘C’ for Celsius, ‘F’ for Fahrenheit, and ‘K’ for Kelvin.

The following conversion formulas between units apply:

Celsius to Fahrenheit: F = C * 9/5 + 32
Fahrenheit to Celsius: C = (F - 32) * 5/9
Celsius to Kelvin: K = C + 273.15
Kelvin to Celsius: C = K - 273.15
Fahrenheit to Kelvin: K = (F - 32) * 5/9 + 273.15
Kelvin to Fahrenheit: F = (K - 273.15) * 9/5 + 32







TippSample Solution: Defining Functions Exercises







Palindrome Exercise


# 1.
def is_palindrome(text):
    text = text.lower()
    return text == text[::-1]

# string to test
test_string = "racecar"
if is_palindrome(test_string):
    print(f"Yes, {test_string} is a palindrome")
else:
    print(f"No, {test_string} is not a palindrome")

Yes, racecar is a palindrome



Exercise Fibonacci


# 2.
def fibonacci(n):

    # the special cases must be considered:
    if n <= 0:
        return []
    elif n == 1:
        return [0]

    # after considering the two special cases, the list always starts as follows:
    fibonacci_sequence = [0, 1]
    
    while len(fibonacci_sequence) < n:
        next_number = fibonacci_sequence[-1] + fibonacci_sequence[-2]
        fibonacci_sequence.append(next_number)
    
    return fibonacci_sequence

fibonacci(8)

[0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13]



Exercise Encryption


Using ListsUsing ord() and chr()



# 3.
def encrypt(text):

    # in six variables, the normal and the reversed alphabet as well as the digits 0-9 are defined
    abc = "abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz"
    abc_2 = "zyxwvutsrqponmlkjihgfedcba"
    ABC = "ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ"
    ABC_2 = "ZYXWVUTSRQPONMLKJIHGFEDCBA"
    digits = "0123456789"
    digits_2 = "9876543210"

    new_string = ""

    for char in text:
        if char in ABC: # uppercase letters
            position = ABC.index(char)
            new_character = ABC_2[position]

            new_string += new_character
        elif char in abc: # lowercase letters
            position = abc.index(char)
            new_character = abc_2[position]

            new_string += new_character   
        elif char in digits: # digits
            position = digits.index(char)
            new_character = digits_2[position]

            new_string += new_character                    
        else: # other characters are kept unchanged
            new_string += char
    
    return new_string
    
# message to be encrypted:
secret_message = 'This is the secret: <*)))><'
encoded_text = encrypt(secret_message)
print(encoded_text)

# the simplest way to decrypt the encrypted message is to apply the same function again to the encrypted message
decoded_text = encrypt(encoded_text)
print(decoded_text)

Gsrh rh gsv hvxivg: <*)))><
This is the secret: <*)))><





def encrypt(a):
    a = list(a)
    # print(a)
    b = []  # output list
    
    for i in a:
        # print(i, ord(i))
        b.append(ord(i))
    # print(b)
            
    # invert: a = 97, z = 122, A = 65, Z = 90, 0 = 48, 9 = 57
    # max - value + min
    
    for i in range(len(b)):
        if b[i] >= 65 and b[i] <= 90:  # uppercase letters
            b[i] = chr(90 - b[i] + 65)
        elif b[i] >= 97 and b[i] <= 122:  # lowercase letters
            b[i] = chr(122 - b[i] + 97)
        elif b[i] >= 48 and b[i] <= 57:  # digits
            b[i] = chr(57 - b[i] + 48) 
        else:  # other characters
            b[i] = chr(b[i])
            
    return ''.join(b)

print(encoded_text)
print(encrypt(a=encoded_text))

Gsrh rh gsv hvxivg: <*)))><
This is the secret: <*)))><






Temperature Converter


# 4.
def convert_temperature(value, from_unit, to_unit):

    # if statements to check which unit the value to be converted is in.
    # Depending on which branch is triggered, the value is converted
    # to the target unit using additional if statements
    
    # if statement for original value in Celsius:
    if from_unit == "C":
        if to_unit == "F":
            return value * 9/5 + 32
        elif to_unit == "K":
            return value + 273.15
        elif to_unit == "C":
            return value
    
    # if statement for original value in Fahrenheit:
    if from_unit == "F":
        if to_unit == "C":
            return (value - 32) * 5/9
        elif to_unit == "K":
            return (value - 32) * 5/9 + 273.15
        elif to_unit == "F":
            return value
    
    # if statement for original value in Kelvin:
    if from_unit == "K":
        if to_unit == "C":
            return value - 273.15
        elif to_unit == "F":
            return (value - 273.15) * 9/5 + 32
        elif to_unit == "K":
            return value

    # return None for invalid units    
    return None

test_temp = convert_temperature(37, "C", "F")
print(f"37°C in Fahrenheit is: {test_temp}°F")

37°C in Fahrenheit is: 98.6°F


Sample solution by Marc Sönnecken and Maik Poetzsch




  (Arnold (2023))
